statement: Montenegro, with a population of approximately 620,000 (comparable to Iceland), opened EU accession negotiations in June 2012 — making it the most advanced Western Balkans candidate. As of 2025, all 33 negotiation chapters have been opened but only 3 provisionally closed, illustrating the difficulty of the accession process even for willing candidates. Montenegro's main challenges are rule of law, judicial reform, and organised crime — issues largely irrelevant to Iceland. However, Montenegro's experience shows that small countries face capacity constraints in managing 33 parallel negotiation chapters simultaneously. Montenegro's small civil service struggles with the administrative burden, a challenge Iceland (with an even smaller bureaucracy) would share.
caveats: Montenegro's slow progress reflects specific political and governance challenges unrelated to Iceland's situation. Iceland's 2010–2013 negotiation experience showed faster progress: 27 chapters opened and 11 provisionally closed in 3 years, reflecting Iceland's high alignment with EU standards through the EEA. Resuming negotiations from the 2013 position could shorten the timeline significantly.

related_entries.statement: Norway held EU membership referendums in 1972 (on EEC membership) and 1994 (on EU membership), both resulting in narrow 'No' votes: 53.5% No in 1972 and 52.2% No in 1994. Key issues included fisheries, agriculture, national sovereignty, and regional policy. Urban areas voted Yes while rural and northern areas voted No.

Iceland applied for EU membership on 16 July 2009, during the aftermath of the financial crisis, under a Social Democrat-led government. Accession negotiations opened in July 2010 and progressed through 33 acquis chapters, of which 27 were opened and 11 provisionally closed by 2013. The most contentious chapters — fisheries (Chapter 13), agriculture (Chapter 11), and free movement of capital (Chapter 4) — remained unresolved. Following the 2013 election, the incoming centre-right coalition suspended negotiations. In March 2015, the government formally asked the EU to remove Iceland from the list of candidate countries. The 2024 Althingi vote for a binding referendum effectively reopened the question without restarting formal negotiations.

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Ísland á mjög litla stjórnsýslu og ef farið verður í aðlögunarviðræður fer allt stjórnkerfið á hliðina, og kostnaðarmat hefur ekki verið lagt fram.
